context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my international development planning class about trade openness, growth, and labor standards. I’m not totally sure how to answer the “is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth?” part without oversimplifying it. Could you help me figure out a clear position?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to start with. Before we dig in — what do you already know or think about the relationship between trade openness and economic growth? Any examples or cases come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh and asking for help forming a position on trade openness and growth. Rather than scaffolding or giving them a direction, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior thinking first. A single open question will help me gauge where they are and let them arrive at their own position.

exchanges.student: I think trade openness can help growth because countries get bigger markets and more investment, but I’m pretty sure it’s not automatic. China comes to mind because it opened a lot and grew fast, but that feels too simple since the state was heavily involved too.
exchanges.tutor: You're already onto something important — you said it's 'not automatic.' What do you think needs to be in place for trade openness to actually translate into growth? What might be missing in cases where it doesn't work?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shown good initial intuition - they recognize both the potential benefits of trade openness AND that state involvement complicates the picture. This is actually a sophisticated starting point. I should affirm the direction of their thinking and push them to develop the tension they've already identified, specifically around the 'not automatic' part, using the Socratic method to get them to think about what conditions matter.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing institutions matter, and maybe state capacity too, but I’m a little fuzzy on how to say that clearly. Like, if a country opens to trade without infrastructure or protections for workers, then maybe the gains don’t spread much or growth is unstable?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid line of thinking. Now, does the historical record support it? Can you think of any cases — beyond China — where countries opened to trade and it went well, or didn't go well, that might help you test that idea?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a nuanced position connecting institutions, state capacity, and distributional outcomes. This is solid thinking. I should affirm the direction and push them to connect this to the historical record, which is explicitly part of the prompt. This will help them move from abstract reasoning to evidence-based argument, which is what the reflection requires.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of stuck on examples. Maybe some Latin American countries liberalized and didn’t get the same results people expected? I’m not confident enough to use that without a clearer hint.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct about Latin America is worth pursuing. What do you recall about the economic policies many Latin American countries adopted in the 1980s and 90s? Even a rough memory of what happened is a good starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with Latin America but is asking for a hint, essentially asking me to give them the answer. I should not do that. Instead, I can acknowledge their instinct is right and push them to think about what they do know about Latin American trade liberalization, perhaps through a guiding question about what policies were applied there in the 80s and 90s. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: I think a lot of them did structural adjustment, privatization, and trade liberalization, and the results were mixed? I vaguely remember growth not being as strong as promised, and inequality staying high, but I’m not sure how to connect that back to the prompt without sounding too broad.
exchanges.tutor: You have the pieces. So given what happened in Latin America versus China — both opened to trade but with very different state roles and outcomes — what does that tell you about whether trade openness and growth are *directly* correlated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has solid recall of the Washington Consensus era policies and their outcomes. They're now worried about being too broad, which is a good metacognitive concern. I should push them to make the connection explicit themselves rather than helping them do it — specifically, how does this historical case speak to the 'direct correlation' question in the prompt? One targeted question should do it.

exchanges.student: I think it suggests the relationship isn’t direct at all. I’d probably say trade openness can support growth, but the historical record shows outcomes depend on how opening is managed, the role of the state, and existing institutions, not just openness by itself.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-reasoned position — good. Now your reflection has several other questions to address too. Which one do you want to tackle next: the shift toward bilateral and regional trade deals, labor standards, or China and India's development strategies?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a clear, defensible thesis on the first part of the prompt through their own reasoning. This is a good moment to acknowledge their progress and redirect them to the other parts of the prompt, which they haven't touched yet — bilateral/regional trade arrangements, labor rights, China and India's strategies, and who monitors labor standards. I should move them forward without dwelling further on what they've already worked out.

exchanges.student: The bilateral and regional trade deals part is confusing me most. I’m not sure if I’m supposed to argue they help international trade by making agreements easier, or hurt it by fragmenting the system.
exchanges.tutor: Does it have to be one or the other? What happens to countries that aren't part of a given bilateral or regional deal — how does that affect the broader multilateral trading system you'd compare it to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is framing this as a binary choice, which is a common but limiting approach. The prompt actually asks whether bilateral/regional arrangements are *compatible* with multilateral trade AND with labor rights — two separate sub-questions. I should push them to think about the tension rather than resolve it prematurely, and also nudge them toward the labor rights dimension they're ignoring.

exchanges.student: I think it can be both, which is where I get tripped up. Regional or bilateral deals might deepen trade among members, but they can also exclude other countries and weaken a universal multilateral system, so I’m not sure how to phrase that cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the tension right — don't worry about phrasing for now, that's your job when you write. But notice the prompt also asks whether these deals are compatible with *labor rights*. What do you think — do bilateral or regional trade agreements typically strengthen or weaken labor protections?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the tension and is now stuck on how to phrase it. But I should not help them with language — my rules explicitly say I cannot suggest language. Instead, I should redirect them to the second part of that sub-question which they haven't touched: are these arrangements compatible with labor rights? This moves the conversation forward and keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: I think they can strengthen them if labor clauses are built in, but I’m skeptical because enforcement seems uneven. I’m also not clear on who actually monitors labor standards, so that part is still pretty confusing to me.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on enforcement. On monitoring — think broadly: what kinds of actors exist at the international level that could oversee labor standards? And do you think any of them have real enforcement power?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has touched on enforcement skepticism, which is sharp. They're now asking about who monitors labor standards, which is directly in the prompt. Rather than explaining this, I should ask them to think about what kinds of actors could plausibly play that role — this will help them arrive at the answer themselves. We're also getting close to the end of the planned conversation length, so I should keep things moving.

exchanges.student: I think the ILO is one, right? Maybe NGOs and unions also track abuses, but I’m guessing they don’t all have the same enforcement power, and that’s the part I need help sorting out.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, the ILO is the main one — and your instinct about uneven enforcement power is worth developing in your writing. You've now worked through most of the prompt. Before wrapping up, you haven't touched China and India's strategies yet. Can you take a first stab at connecting their approaches to trade openness and labor standards, given what you already said about China earlier?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the ILO, NGOs, and unions as key actors. They're asking me to sort out the enforcement power question for them, but I should not do that — they need to reason through it themselves. More importantly, we're at 10 exchanges and the student hasn't addressed China and India's strategies yet, which is a significant part of the prompt. I should acknowledge their good thinking, note the enforcement gap is a valid point to develop in their writing, and push them to now integrate everything into a written attempt since we've covered most of the ground.
